name: YHWH
shortDescription: YHWH is the principal name for God used in the Hebrew Bible, traditionally vocalized as Yahweh or Jehovah. He is considered the God of Israel and the ultimate authority in Judaism.
description: YHWH, often referred to as the Tetragrammaton, is the most sacred name of God in Judaism. Its origin is debated, but it's generally understood to derive from the Hebrew verb 'to be,' implying existence, becoming, or causing to be. YHWH is not merely a name, but a revelation of God's essential nature: eternal, self-sufficient, and actively involved in the world. He is the creator of the universe, the lawgiver, and the covenant maker with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. His role encompasses justice, mercy, and redemption. While depictions of YHWH are forbidden, He is described through His actions and interactions with humanity, particularly through prophets like Isaiah, Jeremiah, and others. The name YHWH is considered so holy that observant Jews traditionally avoid pronouncing it, substituting it with Adonai (Lord) or HaShem (The Name). The concept of YHWH evolves throughout the Hebrew Bible, from a tribal deity to a universal God, emphasizing monotheism and ethical behavior. The relationship between YHWH and humanity is central to Jewish theology, characterized by both divine command and divine love.
epithets: The Holy One, Blessed be He

sources.title: Ancient Israel: From Abraham to the Roman Destruction of the Temple
sources.author: Hershel Shanks

worshipPractices.name: Prayer
worshipPractices.description: Prayer is a central aspect of worship, often directed towards YHWH with requests, praise, and expressions of gratitude. Specific prayers are recited during daily services, holidays, and personal moments of reflection. The Shema, a declaration of God's oneness, is a core prayer recited twice daily.

worshipPractices.name: Torah Study
worshipPractices.description: Studying the Torah and other Jewish texts is considered an act of worship, allowing individuals to understand God's will and commandments. This practice involves analyzing, interpreting, and applying the teachings of the scriptures to daily life. It fosters a deeper connection with YHWH through intellectual and spiritual engagement.

worshipPractices.name: Observance of Commandments (Mitzvot)
worshipPractices.description: Following the commandments outlined in the Torah is a fundamental aspect of worship. These mitzvot encompass various aspects of life, including dietary laws (kashrut), ethical behavior, and ritual observances. By fulfilling these commandments, adherents demonstrate their devotion and commitment to YHWH's divine law.

festivals.name: Passover (Pesach)
festivals.description: Celebrates the Exodus from Egypt, commemorating God's deliverance of the Israelites from slavery. The Seder meal is a central tradition, involving the retelling of the Exodus story, eating symbolic foods, and reciting prayers. It emphasizes themes of freedom, redemption, and divine intervention.

festivals.name: Shavuot
festivals.description: Commemorates the giving of the Torah to Moses on Mount Sinai. Synagogues often read the Ten Commandments, and it's customary to study Torah throughout the night. The holiday symbolizes the covenant between God and the Jewish people and the acceptance of divine law.

festivals.name: Rosh Hashanah
festivals.description: Marks the Jewish New Year, a time for introspection, repentance, and renewal. The shofar (ram's horn) is sounded to awaken the soul and call for reflection. Prayers focus on God's sovereignty and judgment, emphasizing themes of creation, remembrance, and divine providence.

festivals.name: Yom Kippur
festivals.description: The Day of Atonement, the holiest day of the year, dedicated to repentance and seeking forgiveness from God. Observance includes fasting, prayer, and refraining from work, focusing on spiritual purification and reconciliation with YHWH and others. It emphasizes themes of atonement, forgiveness, and renewal.

cultural.modernLegacy: YHWH's influence is prevalent in ethical monotheism, shaping moral values and concepts of justice in Western cultures. The concept of a single, all-powerful God continues to inspire faith, hope, and social activism. Artistic representations, though often symbolic due to the prohibition against depicting God, explore themes of creation, covenant, and divine presence in literature, music, and film.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Ra, the sun god, is often considered the supreme deity and creator in Egyptian mythology, embodying power and essential life force, similar to YHWH's role as the ultimate source of existence.
Zeus, king of the gods, wields immense power and authority, representing a singular, dominant force in the pantheon, analogous to YHWH's supreme position and governance.
Odin, the Allfather, is revered for his wisdom, power, and influence over fate, reflecting a god of ultimate knowledge and rulership, akin to YHWH's omniscient and sovereign nature.
Brahman represents the ultimate, unchanging reality underlying all existence in Hinduism, similar to YHWH as the fundamental source and sustainer of all that is.

associations.name: Fire
associations.significance: Fire represents divine presence, purification, and the transformative power of God, as seen in the burning bush.

associations.name: One
associations.significance: YHWH represents the singular, indivisible nature of God, emphasizing monotheism.

associations.name: Light
associations.significance: Light symbolizes divine illumination, guidance, and the dispelling of darkness, representing God's wisdom and truth.

associations.name: Covenant
associations.significance: YHWH establishes covenants, representing a binding agreement and relationship with humanity, demonstrating faithfulness and promise.

associations.name: Eternity
associations.significance: YHWH's name, derived from 'to be,' suggests timeless existence and being beyond the confines of past, present, and future.

associations.name: Shekinah
associations.significance: The Shekinah, or divine presence, is associated with YHWH, representing God's dwelling among and connection to the world.

associations.name: Wind
associations.significance: Wind, or Ruach, signifies the breath of God, the spirit animating creation and bestowing divine inspiration.

companions.name: The Angels
companions.description: Angels serve as messengers and agents of YHWH, carrying out divine will and acting as intermediaries between the divine and human realms. They are considered extensions of God's power and presence.

companions.name: The Ark of the Covenant
companions.description: The Ark served as a physical representation of YHWH's presence among the Israelites. It housed the tablets of the Law and symbolized the covenant between God and his people.

companions.name: Moses
companions.description: Moses is considered a key prophet and leader, chosen by YHWH to lead the Israelites out of slavery in Egypt and to receive the Torah at Mount Sinai. He acts as an intermediary and delivers God's laws and commandments to the people.

companions.name: The Shekhinah
companions.description: The Shekhinah represents the divine presence or dwelling of YHWH, particularly in the Tabernacle and later in the Temple. It is often described as a feminine aspect of God, symbolizing God's immanence and connection with the world.

extendedContent.title: Origins and Revelation of the Name
extendedContent.content:
The origin of the name YHWH is shrouded in mystery, with its first explicit appearance in the Torah during the episode of the Burning Bush in Exodus 3. When Moses asks God for His name so he can identify Him to the Israelites, God responds with "Ehyeh asher Ehyeh" (אֶהְיֶה אֲשֶׁר אֶהְיֶה), often translated as "I Am That I Am," and then instructs Moses to tell the people that "Ehyeh" (I Am) has sent him. Subsequently, God reveals Himself as YHWH, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. This revelation is pivotal, signifying a shift from a more distant, patriarchal deity to a personal God actively involved in the fate of the Israelites. The connection between "Ehyeh" and YHWH suggests a divine self-definition rooted in being and becoming, emphasizing God's eternal presence and ongoing role in creation. 

Scholars debate whether the name YHWH was entirely unknown before this revelation. Some suggest it may have been known but not widely used, or that its significance was fundamentally transformed by the encounter at the Burning Bush. The very act of revealing the name underscores God's desire for a covenantal relationship with His people, offering them a direct link to the divine. This is further emphasized by the prohibition against misusing the name, reflecting its sacredness and power.

The esoteric interpretations within Kabbalah delve even deeper, viewing the four letters of YHWH (Yod, Heh, Vav, Heh) as representing different aspects of God's being and the structure of the universe itself. Each letter is associated with a different sephirah, or emanation of divine energy, further highlighting the profound significance attributed to the name beyond its literal meaning.

extendedContent.title: Worship, Temple, and the Sacred Name
extendedContent.content:
In ancient Israel, the Temple in Jerusalem served as the central location for the worship of YHWH. It was considered His earthly dwelling place, the locus of divine presence, symbolized by the Ark of the Covenant housed within the Holy of Holies. Sacrifices, prayers, and festivals were all directed towards YHWH, acknowledging His sovereignty and seeking His favor. The Temple priests, particularly the High Priest, held a unique role in maintaining the sanctity of the Temple and leading the rituals.

The uttering of the name YHWH was restricted, particularly within the Temple context. It was believed that the High Priest alone could pronounce the name audibly, and only once a year on Yom Kippur, the Day of Atonement, during the most sacred part of the service. This practice stemmed from a deep reverence for the name and a fear of profaning it. Outside of the Temple, the name was generally replaced with euphemisms like Adonai (Lord) or HaShem (the Name). This avoidance reflects a broader cultural attitude of protecting the divine from human misuse or trivialization.

Following the destruction of the Second Temple in 70 CE, the sacrificial system ceased, and the synagogue became the primary place of Jewish worship. While the Temple liturgy contained the explicit pronunciation of YHWH (by the High Priest on Yom Kippur), the synagogue service continued the tradition of substituting Adonai or HaShem. The absence of the Temple emphasized the importance of prayer, study, and ethical behavior as means of connecting with YHWH. The emphasis shifted from physical rituals to internal devotion, maintaining the reverence for the name even in its unspoken form.

extendedContent.title: Iconography, Representation, and Aniconism
extendedContent.content:
Judaism, unlike many ancient religions, is fundamentally aniconic, meaning it prohibits the creation of graven images to represent God. This stems from the Second Commandment: "You shall not make for yourself a carved image, or any likeness of anything that is in heaven above, or that is in the earth beneath, or that is in the water under the earth." (Exodus 20:4). This prohibition extends to YHWH, who is considered beyond human comprehension and thus unrepresentable in physical form. The focus is on experiencing God through His word, His actions in history, and through the study of Torah.

While direct representations of YHWH are forbidden, indirect symbols sometimes appear in Jewish art and literature. These symbols, however, are not meant to depict God literally but rather to evoke His presence or attributes. Examples include the burning bush, representing the divine revelation to Moses, or the tablets of the Law, symbolizing God's covenant with Israel. In Kabbalistic traditions, geometric shapes like the Tree of Life are used to illustrate the structure of the divine realm and God's attributes, but these are highly abstract and not intended as literal depictions.

The aversion to iconography reflects a belief that any attempt to capture the divine essence in a physical form would inevitably limit and distort it. God is understood as transcendent, existing beyond the boundaries of human perception. The emphasis on aniconism fosters a deeper engagement with God through intellectual and spiritual means, encouraging individuals to seek understanding through study, prayer, and ethical action rather than relying on visual representations.

extendedContent.title: YHWH's Role in Cosmic Order and Creation
extendedContent.content:
According to Jewish mythology and theology, YHWH is the sole creator of the universe, as described in the Book of Genesis. He brought order out of chaos, forming the heavens and the earth, the seas and the dry land, and all living creatures. This act of creation establishes YHWH as the ultimate source of all existence and the sustainer of the cosmos. The creation narrative emphasizes God's power, wisdom, and benevolence in shaping the world for human habitation.

Beyond the initial act of creation, YHWH is also believed to actively maintain cosmic order. He governs the natural world, controlling the seasons, the weather, and the cycles of life and death. Miracles, such as the parting of the Red Sea, are seen as interventions by YHWH to disrupt the natural order for specific purposes, demonstrating His continuing involvement in human affairs. The concept of divine providence underscores the belief that YHWH guides and protects His people, ensuring the ultimate fulfillment of His promises.

Furthermore, YHWH is the source of morality and justice. He established the covenant with Abraham, promising to bless him and his descendants if they remained faithful to Him. The giving of the Torah at Mount Sinai represents the formalization of this covenant, outlining the laws and commandments that the Israelites are obligated to follow. By adhering to these laws, individuals and the community as a whole can maintain a harmonious relationship with YHWH and contribute to the establishment of a just and righteous society. Therefore, YHWH's role extends beyond creation and sustenance to encompass the moral and ethical dimensions of human existence.

subtitle: The God of Israel, Being itself, Source of all life
